book_claims.claim: King mackerel should be avoided due to high mercury content
book_claims.mechanism: High methylmercury levels in large mackerel species due to bioaccumulation up the food chain

book_claims.claim: Top vitamin B12 fish source (14 mcg per 75 g cooked, 233% DV); excellent omega-3 source (0.90–1.39 g EPA/DHA per 75 g cooked; salted mackerel: 3.43 g EPA/DHA)
book_claims.mechanism: Exceptionally rich in both vitamin B12 and omega-3 EPA/DHA

supplementary_research.finding: Atlantic mackerel (Scomber scombrus) has low mercury levels and high omega-3 content; safe and beneficial unlike king mackerel
Oily fish omega-3s including mackerel EPA/DHA reduce triglycerides and cardiovascular mortality; B12 supports homocysteine reduction

nutrient_highlights.bioavailability_notes: Among the highest marine omega-3 concentrations of any fish; direct anti-inflammatory action
Exceptionally high; far exceeds DV in a single serving
One of the richest dietary sources; fat-soluble, enhanced with dietary fat

synergies.description: Both oily fish with high omega-3 and B12; Atlantic mackerel is a safer mackerel alternative to king mackerel
Acid brightens flavor and may aid digestion of rich oily fish
Mackerel is one of few dietary sources of vitamin D that can meaningfully contribute to daily intake
